content.problemStatement:
The beauty of a sequence of non-negative integers is computed in the following way: for each of its non-empty (not necessarily contiguous) subsequences, compute the XOR of all elements of this subsequence; then, sum up all the XORs you obtained.

Let F(N,B)F(N, B)F(N,B) denotes the number of non-negative integer sequences with length NNN which have beauty BBB. You are given three integers NNN, XXX and MMM. Find the smallest non-negative integer BBB such that F(N,B) mod M=XF(N, B) \bmod M = XF(N,B)modM=X, or determine that there is no such BBB. Since even the smallest such BBB might be very large, compute its remainder modulo 998,244,353998,244,353998,244,353.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T. Each test case contains three space-separated integers N, X, and M.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the smallest valid BBB (modulo 998,244,353), or −1 if there is no valid BBB.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using modular arithmetic and the properties of XOR. We can use the fact that XOR is commutative and associative to simplify the problem. We can also use the fact that XOR of all elements in a subsequence is equal to the XOR of the elements in the subsequence. We can use these properties to reduce the problem to a simpler form and solve it using modular arithmetic.
